content.text: An opportunity backlog without a scoring model is a list. With one, it becomes a decision-making tool. Scoring transforms the outputs of ideation workshops — often 30–80 use case candidates across multiple value streams — into a ranked, tiered backlog that the programme PMO, CoE Lead, and executive sponsor can use to allocate discovery capacity, engineering investment, and change management attention. The challenge is building a scoring model that is objective enough to compare use cases across business units, but flexible enough to reflect the organisation's actual context.

attrs.alt: Five scoring dimensions for GenAI opportunity backlog prioritisation

content.text: Why Simple Two-Axis Scoring Is Insufficient
The Impact-Feasibility 2×2 matrix used in ideation workshops is a useful first filter. It is not sufficient for portfolio-level prioritisation because:
'Feasibility' conflates three distinct risks

— Data readiness, technical complexity, and change management complexity are independent. A use case can have clean data, simple technology, and catastrophic change management risk. Collapsing these into one 'feasibility' axis hides the specific blocking factor.
'Impact' conflates business value and strategic alignment

— A use case with high operational cost savings may be low strategic priority if it does not align to the enterprise GenAI strategy pillars. Separating these dimensions produces a more defensible ranking.
Risk is not captured
— A High-Impact/High-Feasibility use case with Critical regulatory risk should not be in Tier 1. Risk must be a scoring input, not an afterthought.
No mechanism for blocked items

— A use case that depends on a platform capability not yet built should be visible in the backlog (as a future commitment) but not competing for current-cycle delivery slots. Standard 2×2 matrices have no blocking mechanism.
The Five-Dimension Scoring Model
The model uses five weighted dimensions, each scored 1–5. The dimensions are independent — a scorer should be able to assign each without reference to the others.
Dimension 1: Business Impact (weight 35%)
What is the quantified business outcome if this use case works as designed? Score against the investment case baseline:
Score
Business Impact Descriptor
Directly drives ≥10% improvement in a tracked business outcome (revenue, cost, NPS, risk loss rate)
Drives 5–10% improvement in a tracked business outcome
Drives 2–5% improvement, or improves a process metric with clear but indirect business linkage
Improves a process metric; business outcome linkage is plausible but not quantified
Productivity or convenience improvement for internal users; no quantified business outcome linkage
Dimension 2: Strategic Fit (weight 20%)
How closely does this use case align to the enterprise GenAI strategy pillars and the priority value streams identified by the ESC?
Score
Strategic Fit Descriptor
Directly implements a named priority from the GenAI strategy; ESC has explicitly approved this use case class
Strongly aligned to a GenAI strategy pillar; contributes to a priority value stream
Aligned to a GenAI strategy pillar; not in a named priority value stream
Loosely aligned; contributes to a department's AI ambition but not to enterprise strategy pillars
No explicit alignment to enterprise GenAI strategy; team-level initiative
Dimension 3: Data Readiness (weight 20%)
Is the data needed to build and sustain this use case available, labelled, and accessible?
Score
Data Readiness Descriptor
Clean, labelled data available now; data access approved; ETL pipeline exists; no PII/GDPR complications
Data available; minor labelling work required (< 4 weeks); data access in progress
Data exists but requires significant cleaning, labelling, or enrichment (1–3 months); no access blockers
Data partially available; gaps require new data collection or third-party sourcing
Data not currently available; requires a data acquisition programme before use case can proceed
Dimension 4: Technical Feasibility (weight 15%)
How complex is the technical implementation given the team's current skills and platform capabilities?
Score
Technical Feasibility Descriptor
Standard GenAI pattern on existing platform; team has delivered similar use cases before
Minor extensions to existing platform or models; skills gap is small and fillable in < 4 weeks
Requires new model type or integration; skills gap is fillable within the programme timeline
Significant technical risk; requires external expertise or new platform capability not yet built
State-of-the-art research area; implementation risk is high; no comparable delivered reference
Dimension 5: Change Feasibility (weight 10%)
How significant is the process, cultural, and role-change impact on the people who will use this AI system?
Score
Change Feasibility Descriptor
AI operates in background; minimal process change; no role scope change; end-users are internal tech-savvy staff
Moderate process change; limited role scope change; strong business owner sponsorship in place
Significant process change; some role scope change; business owner willing to drive adoption
Major process redesign; roles materially affected; change management plan needed before delivery
Transformational change to roles or customer-facing processes; requires regulatory or union engagement
Risk Penalty
After the weighted score is calculated, a risk penalty is subtracted based on the assessed risk level of the use case:
Risk Level
Penalty
Description
Low
No material regulatory, model, or ethical risk identified; standard governance applies
Medium
One risk domain requires elevated monitoring (e.g., model drift in a regulated decision context)
High
Multiple risk domains elevated; risk mitigation plan required before delivery approval
Critical
Regulatory pre-clearance required, or use case involves protected characteristics in high-stakes decisions; must not enter delivery queue until cleared

The risk penalty is not a veto — a Critical use case can still appear in the backlog at a lower tier. It is the signal that unblocking it requires dedicated risk and compliance work before engineering investment is justified.
Scoring Model Implementation

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

ImpactPattern = Literal["Augment", "Automate", "Transform", "Insight"]

WEIGHTS = {
    "business_impact":    0.35,
    "strategic_fit":      0.20,
    "data_readiness":     0.20,
    "tech_feasibility":   0.15,
    "change_feasibility": 0.10,
}

RISK_PENALTY = {"Low": 0.0, "Medium": 0.25, "High": 0.75, "Critical": 1.5}

@dataclass
class BacklogItem:
    id: str
    title: str
    value_stream: str
    pattern: ImpactPattern
    # Scores: 1 (very low) to 5 (very high)
    business_impact: int
    strategic_fit: int
    data_readiness: int
    tech_feasibility: int
    change_feasibility: int
    risk_level: Literal["Low", "Medium", "High", "Critical"] = "Medium"
    blocked_by: str = ""   # dependency that must resolve first

    @property
    def weighted_score(self) -> float:
        raw = (
            self.business_impact    * WEIGHTS["business_impact"] +
            self.strategic_fit      * WEIGHTS["strategic_fit"] +
            self.data_readiness     * WEIGHTS["data_readiness"] +
            self.tech_feasibility   * WEIGHTS["tech_feasibility"] +
            self.change_feasibility * WEIGHTS["change_feasibility"]
        )
        return round(raw - RISK_PENALTY[self.risk_level], 3)

    @property
    def is_blocked(self) -> bool:
        return bool(self.blocked_by)

    @property
    def delivery_tier(self) -> str:
        s = self.weighted_score
        if s >= 3.5: return "Tier 1 — Immediate"
        if s >= 2.5: return "Tier 2 — Next Cycle"
        if s >= 1.5: return "Tier 3 — Future"
        return "Tier 4 — Deprioritise"

def rank_backlog(items: list[BacklogItem]) -> list[BacklogItem]:
    unblocked = [i for i in items if not i.is_blocked]
    blocked   = [i for i in items if i.is_blocked]
    return (
        sorted(unblocked, key=lambda i: i.weighted_score, reverse=True) +
        sorted(blocked,   key=lambda i: i.weighted_score, reverse=True)
    )

# Sample backlog
backlog = [
    BacklogItem("UC-001", "FNOL auto-triage",
                "Insurance Claims", "Automate",
                business_impact=5, strategic_fit=4, data_readiness=4,
                tech_feasibility=4, change_feasibility=3, risk_level="Low"),
    BacklogItem("UC-002", "Settlement recommendation",
                "Insurance Claims", "Augment",
                business_impact=4, strategic_fit=5, data_readiness=3,
                tech_feasibility=3, change_feasibility=2, risk_level="Medium"),
    BacklogItem("UC-003", "Fraud propensity scoring",
                "Insurance Claims", "Insight",
                business_impact=5, strategic_fit=5, data_readiness=3,
                tech_feasibility=2, change_feasibility=4, risk_level="Medium"),
    BacklogItem("UC-004", "Policy document summarisation",
                "Underwriting", "Augment",
                business_impact=3, strategic_fit=3, data_readiness=5,
                tech_feasibility=5, change_feasibility=5, risk_level="Low"),
    BacklogItem("UC-005", "Real-time re-pricing engine",
                "Pricing", "Transform",
                business_impact=5, strategic_fit=5, data_readiness=2,
                tech_feasibility=1, change_feasibility=1, risk_level="High",
                blocked_by="Requires regulatory pre-clearance from FCA"),
]

ranked = rank_backlog(backlog)
header = f"{'ID':<8} {'Title':<35} {'Score':>6}  {'Tier':<22} {'Blocked?'}"
print(header)
print("-" * 90)
for item in ranked:
    blocked_str = f"Yes ({item.blocked_by[:25]}...)" if item.is_blocked else "No"
    print(f"{item.id:<8} {item.title:<35} {item.weighted_score:>6.3f}  {item.delivery_tier:<22} {blocked_str}")

# ID       Title                               Score  Tier                   Blocked?
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# UC-001   FNOL auto-triage                    4.150  Tier 1 — Immediate     No
# UC-004   Policy document summarisation       4.000  Tier 1 — Immediate     No
# UC-002   Settlement recommendation           3.200  Tier 2 — Next Cycle    No
# UC-003   Fraud propensity scoring            3.200  Tier 2 — Next Cycle    No
# UC-005   Real-time re-pricing engine         2.400  Tier 2 — Next Cycle    Yes (Requires regulatory pre-clearance...)
Weight Profiles by Organisational Context

The default weights reflect a balanced organisation at GenAI maturity Level 2–3. Organisations in different contexts should adjust weights at the start of each scoring cycle and document the rationale. Weight changes must be approved by the CoE Lead and programme PMO — preventing individual BUs from gaming scores by advocating for weight profiles that favour their own use cases.
Backlog Tiers and Review Cadence
Tier
Score Range
Description
Review Cadence
Tier 1 — Immediate
Fund and start discovery sprint within the current planning cycle
Monthly — track progress to delivery
Tier 2 — Next Cycle
Plan for the next planning cycle; monitor for scoring changes
Quarterly — re-score if context changes
Tier 3 — Future
Retain in backlog; revisit when data readiness or strategic fit improves
Semi-annual — check for unblocking events
Tier 4 — Deprioritise
Archive with documented rationale; do not invest further unless context materially changes
Annual — confirm archive decision

Blocked items are tiered by their score but flagged separately. They do not compete for current-cycle delivery capacity. The blocking condition is tracked by the PMO — when the block is resolved (regulatory clearance received, data platform capability shipped), the item is automatically elevated to active competition in its scored tier.
Scoring Governance: Who Scores What
Scores are not assigned by a single person. Dimension ownership must be explicit:
Dimension
Score Assigned By
Challenge Right Held By
Business Impact
Business owner of the value stream
CFO / Programme Sponsor (if score seems inflated)
Strategic Fit
CoE Lead in consultation with programme PMO
ESC (if a use case claims alignment it does not have)
Data Readiness
Data platform lead
CoE ML Engineer (technical challenge to data claims)
Technical Feasibility
CoE ML Engineer
External technical review (if score seems optimistic)
Change Feasibility
BU change management lead
HR / unions for use cases affecting roles at scale
Risk Level
CoE Risk lead
Chief Risk Officer (for Critical classifications)

Scoring sessions are run as structured reviews — not ad hoc conversations. The PMO schedules a 90-minute backlog scoring session at the start of each planning cycle (typically quarterly). Each new use case submitted since the last session is scored by the relevant dimension owners, with the full group present to flag disagreements. Scores are locked in the metric registry at the end of the session.
Re-scoring Triggers
A use case score can change between cycles. Re-scoring outside the quarterly cycle is permitted only when a material change event occurs:
Data readiness resolved
— Data procurement or labelling project completes; data readiness score rises from 2 to 4. If this moves the item from Tier 2 to Tier 1, the PMO may advance it to the current cycle.
Regulatory clearance received
— A Critical risk item is cleared; risk penalty drops from 1.5 to 0.25. This can move a blocked item into active delivery competition.
Business context change
— A competitor launches an AI product in the same space; strategic fit jumps. Or a business restructuring removes the value stream; business impact drops to 1.
Technical breakthrough
— A new foundation model or platform capability eliminates a technical blocker; tech feasibility rises.

Re-scoring requests are submitted to the PMO with documented evidence for the changed dimension. The CoE Lead approves or rejects within 5 business days. Approved re-scores are applied immediately and reflected in the next delivery capacity allocation.
Portfolio View: Balancing the Backlog
A ranked backlog answers 'what should we do next?' A portfolio view answers 'is the shape of what we are doing strategically coherent?' The portfolio view cuts the backlog across three axes:
By AI Pattern

A healthy GenAI portfolio is not dominated by any single pattern. Over-concentration in Automate use cases increases dependency on model accuracy and creates workforce displacement risk. Over-concentration in Augment use cases may leave significant efficiency gains on the table. The recommended target distribution for a mature enterprise programme:
AI Pattern
Target Portfolio Share
Rationale
Augment
Lowest risk, fastest adoption, highest trust from practitioners; good foundation for model maturity
Automate
Highest efficiency gains; requires mature data and model governance before scaling
Insight
High value but requires investment in decision support culture; do not start here
Transform
Long time to value; reserve for well-funded strategic bets with ESC-level sponsorship
Early-stage programmes (Maturity Level 1–2) should target 60–70% Augment, leaving Automate and Transform for later cycles when model governance and data infrastructure are stronger.
By Value Stream

No single value stream should consume more than 40% of discovery and delivery capacity in a given cycle. Over-concentration creates dependency on one business unit and masks whether the GenAI programme is delivering enterprise-wide value or serving as one team's R&D budget.
By Time Horizon
A well-shaped backlog has use cases across all time horizons:
Now (0–3 months)
— Tier 1 items in active discovery or delivery. These are the programme's evidence base.
Next (3–12 months)
— Tier 2 items being unblocked (data procurement, regulatory pre-engagement) while Tier 1 items deliver.
Later (12+ months)
— Tier 3 items being tracked for context changes that would elevate them. Includes Strategic Bets where capability gaps are being closed.

The PMO publishes a quarterly portfolio view alongside the backlog ranking. The ESC uses this to validate that the programme is diversified and time-balanced — not burning all capacity on near-term Augment use cases while failing to invest in capabilities that unlock future Transform value.
Score Calibration Across Business Units

When multiple business units submit use cases from independent ideation workshops, scores are not automatically comparable. A BU that scores all its candidates as '5 on Business Impact' produces candidates that dominate the backlog regardless of actual relative value. Calibration sessions prevent this.
Inter-BU Calibration Process
After each BU completes its initial scoring, the PMO collects all scores into a single backlog view.
The CoE Lead runs a 2-hour calibration session with one representative from each BU plus the data platform lead.
The session selects 3–5 'anchor' use cases — one high, one medium, one low impact — and agrees on their scores collaboratively. These become reference points for all BU-submitted scores.
BU representatives are asked to re-score their own use cases relative to the anchors. Systematic BU inflation (all scores 4–5 with no 1–2s) triggers a facilitated challenge session.
Final calibrated scores are locked by the CoE Lead. BU representatives may escalate disagreements to the programme sponsor within 5 business days.
Calibration is not about equalising scores across BUs — it is about ensuring that a '4 on Business Impact' means the same thing regardless of which BU submitted the use case.
Anti-Gaming Controls
Without controls, BU leaders will advocate for scores that maximise their use cases' ranking. The following controls reduce gaming risk:
Dimension ownership separation
— No BU owner scores their own use case on Data Readiness or Technical Feasibility. These are scored by the data platform lead and CoE engineer respectively, who have no BU incentive.
Challenge right

— The CoE Lead has explicit authority to challenge any Business Impact score with no documentary evidence. Claims of '5 on Business Impact' without a quantified investment case are reduced to 3 pending evidence.
Audit trail
— The metric registry stores the initial score, any re-score, and the documented reason for each change. This deters post-hoc score manipulation.
Anonymous dot-vote calibration
— In calibration sessions, initial impact scores are revealed simultaneously (not sequentially) to prevent anchoring on the loudest voice.
Summary

Simple two-axis Impact-Feasibility scoring is insufficient for portfolio-level prioritisation. A five-dimension model (Business Impact, Strategic Fit, Data Readiness, Technical Feasibility, Change Feasibility) plus a risk penalty produces a defensible, comparable composite score.

Each dimension is scored by its natural owner — business impact by the value stream owner, data readiness by the data platform lead, technical feasibility by the CoE engineer. No single person assigns all dimensions.
The composite score determines a delivery tier (Tier 1 Immediate through Tier 4 Deprioritise). Blocked items are scored but excluded from active delivery competition until the block resolves.

Weight profiles can be adjusted by organisational context — data-constrained organisations weight data readiness higher; regulated industries weight change feasibility higher. Weight changes require PMO and CoE Lead approval.
Quarterly scoring sessions lock scores into the metric registry. Re-scoring between cycles requires documented evidence of a material change event.
